turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 250, Lloyd Eldon Miller Jr. versus Frank J. Pate, Warden. Mr. Lassers.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, this is a capital case that comes to this Court on habeas corpus. Relief was granted by the District Court of the Northern District of Illinois, Judge Perry, the state took an appeal and the Seventh Circuit reversed and this Court granted certiorari. There are four principal errors that we contend occurred in this trial and I'm going to try cover the four. The case is unique, Your Honors, and that the covenants on exonerating this petitioner of the crime was discovered even before the petitioner himself was arrested. The -- the petitioner was convicted of murder and on Saturday afternoon, November 26th, 1955 in Canton, Illinois at 4:00 in the afternoon and a little eight year old girl was found under a mining car severely injured but still living. She was rushed to a hospital where she dies at 5:47 P.M. that afternoon. At 6:20, while the body was still on the operating table, the coroner arrived and he performed an examination. Now, the coroner himself was a physician. In the course of that examination, he took a vaginal smear and there had been a sex attack on this child and he took the smear and the vaginal smear was sealed in a vial with a surgical tape and this smear together with a mass of other evidence was immediately taken to Springfield which was few miles away and it was taken to the state crime laboratory there. When it arrived there, the man in-charge, a man by the name of Forest literally, had found the vial and it was still sealed and it had on it Janice May, the name of the victim and the date of the crime November 26th, 1955 and the initials of the coroner. He opened the vial and inside was a tissue which he examined. And when he made a close examination, he found that there was a single strand of hair that was adhering to the tissue. The petitioner Lloyd Eldon Miller was arrested the following day on November the 29th, at about six o'clock -- no about eight o'clock in the evening, on a Monday. The crime took place on Saturday and he was arrested on Monday. He was immediately taken to Springfield which was the same place for the state crime laboratory was located and he -- they took him up to the crime laboratory and they took blood sample and this and that and other things and among -- there are other examinations, they took a sample of his pubic hairs. And immediately after that, on the night of the November the 29th, they had an extensive period of interrogation which lasted I believe until one in the morning. He was taken back to his cell on the following night, the night of the 30th, there was another extensive period of examination and he signed a confession shortly after midnight on Wednesday evening which was the second night of the examination. Now, he was tried in Carthage Illinois, in September of 1956 and which was about eight months -- ten months afterwards. And in May, which was several months before the -- before the trial, the -- his then defense attorneys, we were not representing him at the time, filed a motion on the trial court asking, I believe, to make a physical examination of the evidence. And the state came in and they resisted that, and the trial court denied the motion, and they preserved the error in the record and the Supreme Court of Illinois affirmed the denial of the right to make a physical examination of the evidence and the -- they affirmed the conviction.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Specifically what evidence? What -- what evidence did he want physically to examine?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Well, there was --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: The vial?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: The -- there was a physical evidence that was later introduced to the trial. I don't think that they knew themselves exactly what physical evidence there was. But there were various -- there was a -- that there was a pair of shorts which we can talk about later on.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Yes, I just wondered whether the motion referred to anything specific or just generally asked permission to examine all physical evidence.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: I believe that is correct. I assume that's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Did the hair a part of evidence in the trial?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: It was not Your Honor, no. Now, the record -- what?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Offered?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: It was not offered, no, no. Now, the petitioner --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Could I ask -- could I ask you another question?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Was there any proof that he were at any stage in supplementary proceeding, or otherwise, that the pubic hair was actually found in the vagina?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: The -- the record shows that the -- that the physician came and that we thought the vial was there and it have the initials of the coroner, it was still sealed, with the name of the child and the date of the crime, and that this was then sent to the crime laboratory, and that is the extent of the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: In other words, the hair was in the vial?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: The hair was on -- was adhering to a tissue in the vial --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: In the vial?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But the person who actually took the smear never testified that he found a hair in the vagina?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: No, he did not. He have --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And that's only an inference from the fact that the hair was in the vial? That -- that you think it came from the vagina at the time.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Mr. Justice White, we think that the inference on that is just overwhelming.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Yes, but wholly aside of how you characterized it, that's what it rest on.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: That is correct. Now, the -- it is also a fact that this coroner did testify. He was called as a witness by the prosecution and he did testify and he said that when he arrived there, the girl was dead and I believe he said that she was still on the operating table. Now, the -- the counsel for the defendant at the time was permitted to physically to view the physical evidence on one occasion and he subsequently testified in subsequent proceedings.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, what does that mean? Allowed physically to view -- what's that?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Well, he -- he went into the room and the evidence was put on a table before him and he with his co-counsel and they -- they looked. I don't know whether they were allowed to touch or not.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, how does that different from what they sought on --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: What they sought was permission to have a scientific examination made which would involve bringing in specialists in various kinds --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, did they see this after the motion was denied but before trial?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: I believe that's correct, Your Honor. Now, after they --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did they see what before -- before trial but after -- after their --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: They saw a pair of short, a jacket, and a rock, and --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: The vial?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: I think they saw the vial, yes. Now, it --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Was the hair observable?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: I don't know -- I don't know whether the record shows whether it was or it was not. It may have been. Now --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Was it called to their attention that there was such a thing --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: The record --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- in the vial?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: The record does not show that it was ever called to their attention. However, the petitioner testified that he was showing a hair and I will come --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He was shown what?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: -- to that in just a moment.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He was shown what?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: That he was shown a hair. During the course of his interrogation and this was on the second night and it was shortly before he signed a confession, he testified that he was shown a hair, that he was taken into a microscope room which is what he called it, and that he was told that one of his hairs matched the hair from the little -- that was found in the little girl and he said that that -- they told him that he could look at the microscope through himself and then he said that he informed the people present them hairs don't match.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: That -- that what?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Them hairs don't match.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: That -- the petitioner's said that?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor and he -- he so testified at the -- at the hearing on the motion to admit the confession.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well now, what did the -- the police concede that they had shown him a hair during the period that that is --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: There was no denial of this.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: No denial?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: The record on the prosecution side assigned on that issue.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But there's no concession that it was shown?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: It's silent. And I -- I think it's a fair to say that they have conceded, that they showed him the hair and that they told him that the hairs matched.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, when was it -- how long after this was -- that was determined that in fact this was not a hair from his body?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Eight years, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Eight years from --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Eight years, yes sir. It was -- then, what happened was after the affirmance --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, when did the police first know that it was not a hair from his body?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: At the very latest, on December the 7th of 1955 --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: That?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: -- which was -- and we think it's a fair inference from the record that they knew it the very afternoon that the hair was delivered to the crime laboratory which was a day or two after the crime.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, in any event, at the time he was shown the hair and told that it was from his body, the police knew that in fact it was not from his body.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Well, the -- the -- we think that that is the inescapable inference from the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, what I'm trying to get to is, how do we know that police knew that it was not from his body at the time they told him it was? What in the record shows that?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Only -- it's only the inference and based on this fact that the hair was already in the crime laboratory for over 24 hours at the time they made the false statement to him.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: It was there but I'm still uncertain how it is they knew it was not his. When was it determined and by whom was it determined that it was not a hair --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: It was --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- from his body?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: It was determined by his fellow literally in -- and he wrote up an official report --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is he in the crime laboratory?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: He is in the crime laboratory --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: And he wrote an official report, one copy of which went to the prosecuting attorney --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: In which he stated that it was not a hair from his body?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, when was that report prepared?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: It is dated December the 7th, 1955.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And how long -- on what dates was the interrogation?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: On November the 29th and November the 30th. And the hair --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And prior to the date of that report in any event?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Correct, Your Honor and it was -- the hair was delivered to the crime laboratory on the 28th.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, then how do you jump to the conclusion that between the 28th and 30th that it had been discovered it was not a hair from his body and the interrogating officers knew it? How do you --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: All we can say on that, Your Honor, is that either they knew it or that they made a statement to the petitioner with this regard whether it was true or false. Now, we say it doesn't make any difference whether they knew it or whether they -- they told it to him without making the determination.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, on this branch of your case, are you making two points -- one that the police used the hair to get the confession and that that was error?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Fortas.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And two that there was error before the prosecution to fail to advice the counsel?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- or the petitioner that their expert had reported that the hairs were the same?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: -- or not the same?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: So you have those two points?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What did counsel do about this when -- on it, wherein from the petitioner that he had told the interrogators it wasn't his hair?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: The record is silent on that. Now, of course they --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Does that mean they did nothing?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: The record doesn't show that they did anything. But of course they were in a terrible position because what could they do is see that the state never introduced the hair at the trial.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, this -- it would seem to me that if it was a valuable evidence for -- for the defendant now, in this proceeding, it's certainly would have been valuable evidence to him then and if counsel was taking cognizance of what would help him, he would think they would make some kind of an effort to have it determined. There's a fact --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: And if --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- whether it was his or not?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, all they had was what this uneducated cab driver with a two-year high school education at the most told them, he told the investigating authority --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, that's why they have lawyers with more two years education and to take care of his rights in the courtroom that would think.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: That is correct, and that -- that is the reason that they made the -- the request, to have -- to be permitted to make a scientific examination of the evidence and that was denied.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did they -- did they state why they wanted to do that?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did they say it to him it was because the defendant said that was not his hair and that they wanted to examine that particular item of evidence?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: No, the motion does not state that. It does not, no. Now --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Does it refer that the hair, the vial was distinguished in other physical -- No, it does not.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: It does not, Mr. Justice Harlan. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Just one last on that question. Did these lawyers testify at the least the post-conviction or other proceedings?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Yes, they did.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Did they -- did they testify that the motion itself didn't say anything about it? What it was -- they had in mind when they made the motion?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: I don't believe they did and I -- I'm not certain about that but I don't think they did.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, in any event, they certainly didn't say they had in mind they wanted an examination of the hair, did they?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: I don't believe they did. I am not certain of what of state of the record is on that point. Now, the -- then the -- then the next step was there was a habeas corpus proceeding in the Northern District of Illinois, relief was denied and that was affirmed by the Supreme -- by the Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit and that was held up by the other counsel. Then, we came to August of 1963 when a petition for commutation was filed and the -- there was a hearing before the Illinois Pardon and Parole Board and in the course of that hearing there was certain evidence introduced by the prosecution and by the defendants which -- which for the first time cast substantial real doubt on the man's guilt and the -- we were then about two days before the execution, the execution was set for one minute after midnight on August the 23rd and the commutation was denied on August the 20th. So we proceeded in two channels. One was, the date for death that have been sent by the Supreme Court of Illinois which was not been in session --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: When -- when does he have counsel for the first time?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: His parents obtained counsel for him even while he was still being interrogated.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, before he signed the confession?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Yes, that's true. The counsel never saw him until after the confession was signed.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Why -- why not?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: They -- he was held incommunicado. He asked to see --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: In other words, (Voice Overlap) refused to let him see him?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: That is correct and --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But the lawyer tried to see him?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: I believe the record shows that his lawyer inquiries to get to see him and I believe that the -- the first time that his lawyers saw him was on December the 1st which was the morning following the night when he signed the confession.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did I ask you before we adjourned if the doctor who made this -- this test of the material in the -- in the vial testified --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: He did --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- in the case?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: He did testify, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did -- did he say anything about the hair being attached to --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: No, he did not.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Was he asked by counsel from cross-examination whether there was any such thing in the vial?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: He -- there was nothing.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: No mention of it by --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: No mentioned --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- by either side?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: By either side, that is correct. Yes, I believe he testified that the injury were something about the character. So that was the -- we made an application to a justice of the Supreme Court of Illinois in vacation, for a stay, that was denied. We filed a second application for habeas corpus which was the time when the present group of attorneys accepted the case and that was granted by Judge Decker and then the matter was sat down for a full hearing before Judge Perry. And --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess -- we'll recess now Mister.
